\begin{definition}[\langref{CNF}]\label{def:lang_89649e36}
\textbf{Conjunctive Normal Form} \\
A \langref{CNF} is a conjunction of clauses $\bigwedge_{i=1}^mC_i$. A clause is a disjunction of literals $C_i=\bigvee_{j=1}^{k_i}l_j$ where a literal $l_j$ is a variable $x$ or its negation $\lnot x$. For example, $(x \lor y \lor \lnot z)\land (x\lor \lnot y)\land (z\lor w)$.
\end{definition}

\begin{definition}[\langref{cSDD}]\label{def:lang_83e3b023}
\textbf{Compressed Sentential Decision Diagrams} \\
\langref{cSDD} $= \bigcup_T \langfam{cSDD}{T}$. \citep{VanDenBroeck_2015,Darwiche_2011}
\end{definition}

\begin{definition}[\langfam{cSDD}{T}]\label{def:lang_82fa749e}
\textbf{Compressed Sentential Decision Diagrams (wrt a fixed vtree)} \\
\langfam{cSDD}{T} is the subset of \langfam{SDD}{T} that is \emph{compressed}, a restriction that makes the representation canonical (i.e., there is a unique representation for every function). \citep{Darwiche_2011}
\end{definition}

\begin{definition}[\langref{d-DNNF}]\label{def:lang_6c130090}
\textbf{Deterministic Decomposable Negation Normal Form} \\
Boolean circuits with AND and OR gates; literals as inputs; and whose AND gates are \emph{decomposable} (children mention disjoint sets of variables) and whose OR gates are \emph{deterministic} (on any input, at most one child evaluates to $1$).\citep{Darwiche_2001b}
\end{definition}

\begin{definition}[\langref{d-SDNNF}]\label{def:lang_ea9b5299}
\textbf{Deterministic Structured Decomposable Negation Normal Form} \\
\langref{d-SDNNF} $= \bigcup_T$ \langfam{d-SDNNF}{T}. \citep{Pipatsrisawat_2008}
\end{definition}

\begin{definition}[\langfam{d-SDNNF}{T}]\label{def:lang_91f812d0}
\textbf{Deterministic Structured Decomposable Negation Normal Form (wrt a fixed vtree)} \\
For each fixed vtree $T$, a \langfam{d-SDNNF}{T} is a \langref{d-DNNF} that is additionally structured decomposable, respecting the vtree $T$.

Intuitively, all AND gates over the same set of variables must decompose via the same partition, as specified by the vtree.

Equivalently, \langfam{d-SDNNF}{T} formulas are \langfam{SDNNF}{T} formulas that are also \emph{deterministic} (see \langref{d-DNNF}).\citep{Pipatsrisawat_2008}
\end{definition}

\begin{definition}[\langref{dec-DNNF}]\label{def:lang_981b62f0}
\textbf{Decision Decomposable Negation Normal Form} \\
Boolean circuits with AND and OR gates; literals as inputs; and whose AND gates are \emph{decomposable} (children mention disjoint sets of variables) and whose OR gates must appear in the form of \emph{decisions/tests} (subcircuits of the form $(x\land f)\lor (\bar{x}\land g)$ where $x$ is a variable).\citep{darwiche2007language}

Equivalently, \langref{dec-DNNF} formulas are \langref{d-DNNF} formulas whose OR-gates may only appear in \emph{decisions/tests}.

Equivalently, \langref{dec-DNNF} formulas are (up to syntax) \langref{FBDD}{s} that may additionally contain decomposable AND-gates.
\end{definition}

\begin{definition}[\langref{dec-SDNNF}]\label{def:lang_0f27d539}
\textbf{Decision Structured Decomposable Negation Normal Form} \\
\langref{dec-SDNNF} $= \bigcup_T$ \langfam{dec-SDNNF}{T}. \citep{Amarilli_2018}
\end{definition}

\begin{definition}[\langfam{dec-SDNNF}{T}]\label{def:lang_4ae03bc8}
\textbf{Decision Structured Decomposable Negation Normal Form (wrt a fixed vtree)} \\
For each fixed vtree $T$, \langfam{dec-SDNNF}{T} is the subset of \langref{dec-DNNF} that respects $T$.\citep{darwiche2007language,Amarilli_2018}

Equivalently, \langfam{dec-SDNNF}{T} formulas are \langfam{SDNNF}{T} formulas whose OR-gates may only appear in \emph{decisions/tests}.
\end{definition}

\begin{definition}[\langref{DNF}]\label{def:lang_4c204bf3}
\textbf{Disjunctive Normal Form} \\
A \langref{DNF} is a disjunction of terms $\bigvee_{i=1}^mT_i$. A term is a conjunction of literals $T_i=\bigwedge_{j=1}^{k_i}l_j$ where a literal $l_j$ is a variable $x$ or its negation $\lnot x$. For example, $(x \land y \land \lnot z)\lor (x\land \lnot y)\lor (z\land w)$.

Equivalently, a \langref{DNF} is a \langref{DNNF} of depth two with an output OR gate whose children are AND gates over literals.
\end{definition}

\begin{definition}[\langref{DNNF}]\label{def:lang_3bebcab7}
\textbf{Decomposable Negation Normal Form} \\
Boolean circuits with AND and OR gates, literals as inputs, and whose AND gates are \emph{decomposable} (children mention disjoint sets of variables).\citep{Darwiche_2001a} Decomposability may be seen as the analogue in Boolean circuits to the notion of \emph{syntactic multilinearity} in arithmetic circuits.\citep{shpilka2010arithmetic}
\end{definition}

\begin{definition}[\langref{FBDD}]\label{def:lang_684b1ca7}
\textbf{Free Binary Decision Diagram} \\
Binary decision diagrams such that each root-to-sink path tests each variable at most once. Also known as read-once branching programs.\citep{Wegener_2000}
\end{definition}

\begin{definition}[\langref{IP}]\label{def:lang_6ae90adc}
\textbf{Prime Implicants} \\
A \langref{DNF} that is the disjunction of all minimal entailing terms (prime implicants). \citep{Quine_1952}
\end{definition}

\begin{definition}[\langref{MODS}]\label{def:lang_e02902d0}
\textbf{Models} \\
The set of satisfying assignments (models) of the function.\citep{Darwiche_2002}

Equivalently, a suitably restricted \langref{DNF} whose terms are in one-to-one correspondence with the models.
\end{definition}

\begin{definition}[\langref{nFBDD}]\label{def:lang_1df07cc3}
\textbf{Nondeterministic Free Binary Decision Diagram} \\
An \langref{nFBDD} is a \emph{nondeterministic} \langref{FBDD}, i.e., an \langref{FBDD} that is additionally allowed to contain \emph{guess} nodes.\citep{Wegener_2000}

Also known as a nondeterministic read-once branching program.
\end{definition}

\begin{definition}[\langref{NNF}]\label{def:lang_5bf00851}
\textbf{Negation Normal Form} \\
A Boolean circuit with AND and OR gates with literals as inputs (i.e., negations may only appear at leaves). Also called \emph{De Morgan circuits}. \citep{Darwiche_2002}
\end{definition}

\begin{definition}[\langref{nOBDD}]\label{def:lang_d24efe0e}
\textbf{Nondeterministic Ordered Binary Decision Diagram} \\
\langref{nOBDD} $= \bigcup_{<}$ \langfam{nOBDD}{<}. \citep{Wegener_2000}
\end{definition}

\begin{definition}[\langfam{nOBDD}{<}]\label{def:lang_e827cf31}
\textbf{Nondeterministic Ordered Binary Decision Diagram (wrt a fixed variable order)} \\
An \langfam{nOBDD}{<} is a \emph{nondeterministic} \langfam{OBDD}{<}, i.e., an \langfam{OBDD}{<} that is additionally allowed to contain \emph{guess} nodes.\citep{Wegener_2000}

Also known as a nondeterministic oblivious read-once branching program respecting variable order $<$.
\end{definition}

\begin{definition}[\langref{OBDD}]\label{def:lang_b9d72a7c}
\textbf{Ordered Binary Decision Diagram} \\
Binary decision diagrams such that each root-to-sink path tests each variable at most once and the variables appear in the same order.\citep{Bryant_1986}

Also known as oblivious read-once branching programs.\citep{Wegener_2000}

Equivalently, \langref{OBDD} $=\bigcup_{<}$ \langfam{OBDD}{<}.\citep{Darwiche_2002}
\end{definition}

\begin{definition}[\langfam{OBDD}{<}]\label{def:lang_d69995dd}
\textbf{Ordered Binary Decision Diagram (wrt a fixed variable order)} \\
For each fixed variable order $<$, binary decision diagrams such that each root-to-sink path tests each variable at most once and the variables appear in the order $<$.\citep{Bryant_1986}

Equivalently, oblivious read-once branching programs respecting the variable order $<$.\citep{Wegener_2000}

Equivalently, \langfam{OBDD}{<}{s} are \langref{FBDD}{s} required to respect the order $<$ on every root-to-sink path.
\end{definition}

\begin{definition}[\langref{PI}]\label{def:lang_27fffab2}
\textbf{Prime Implicates} \\
The subset of \langref{CNF} where formulas are the conjunction of all minimal entailed clauses (prime implicates). \citep{Quine_1952}.
\end{definition}

\begin{definition}[\langref{SDD}]\label{def:lang_1afefbe2}
\textbf{Sentential Decision Diagram} \\
\langref{SDD} $= \bigcup_T$ \langfam{SDD}{T}. \citep{Darwiche_2011}
\end{definition}

\begin{definition}[\langfam{SDD}{T}]\label{def:lang_9c84a267}
\textbf{Sentential Decision Diagram (wrt a fixed vtree)} \\
\langfam{SDD}{T} generalizes \langfam{OBDD}{<} by allowing decisions, not only on variables, but on sentences (i.e., recursively defined \langref{SDD} formulas).\citep{Darwiche_2011}

Equivalently, an \langfam{SDD}{T} is a \langfam{d-SDNNF}{T} in which determinism is replaced by \emph{strong determinism}, where $\lor$-gates must take the form $ f(x,y) = \bigvee_{i=1}^{m}(p_i(x) \land s_i(y))$, where the $p_i$ are mutually exclusive and exhaustive.
\end{definition}

\begin{definition}[\langref{SDNNF}]\label{def:lang_b13b0d78}
\textbf{Structured Decomposable Negation Normal Form} \\
\langref{SDNNF} $= \bigcup_T$ \langfam{SDNNF}{T}. \citep{Pipatsrisawat_2008}
\end{definition}

\begin{definition}[\langfam{SDNNF}{T}]\label{def:lang_3c803ba1}
\textbf{Structured Decomposable Negation Normal Form (wrt a fixed vtree)} \\
For each fixed vtree $T$, an \langfam{SDNNF}{T} is a \langref{DNNF} that is additionally structured decomposable, respecting the vtree $T$.\citep{Pipatsrisawat_2008}

Intuitively, all AND gates over the same set of variables must decompose via the same partition, as specified by the vtree.
\end{definition}

\begin{definition}[\langref{TDD}]\label{def:lang_d7403a53}
\textbf{Tree Decision Diagram} \\
\langref{TDD} $= \bigcup_T$ \langfam{TDD}{T}. \citep{Capelli_2026}
\end{definition}

\begin{definition}[\langfam{TDD}{T}]\label{def:lang_8cf1da0e}
\textbf{Tree Decision Diagram (wrt a fixed vtree)} \\
For each fixed vtree $T$, a \langfam{TDD}{T} is a deterministic tree decision diagram respecting $T$, as introduced by \citet{Capelli_2026}.
\end{definition}

\begin{definition}[\langref{uFBDD}]\label{def:lang_4e62a038}
\textbf{Unambiguous Free Binary Decision Diagram} \\
The \emph{unambiguous} variant of \langref{nFBDD}.\citep{Wegener_2000} That is, on any input there is at most one accepting sequence of guesses.
\end{definition}

\begin{definition}[\langref{uOBDD}]\label{def:lang_c2df8c2b}
\textbf{Unambiguous Ordered Binary Decision Diagram} \\
The \emph{unambiguous} variant of \langref{nOBDD}.\citep{Wegener_2000} That is, on any input there is at most one accepting sequence of guesses.
\end{definition}

\begin{definition}[\langfam{uOBDD}{<}]\label{def:lang_43a33aec}
\textbf{Unambiguous Ordered Binary Decision Diagram (wrt a fixed variable order)} \\
The \emph{unambiguous} variant of \langfam{nOBDD}{<}.\citep{Wegener_2000} That is, on any input there is at most one accepting sequence of guesses.
\end{definition}
